title: Collimators and Source Changers
narrationScript: A collimator is shielding you aim. Mounted at the end of the guide tube, it surrounds the exposure position with tungsten and leaves open only the port the radiograph needs. The beam through that port is as intense as ever, but every other direction is cut by a factor of sixteen to well over one hundred — and because dose rate falls with the square of distance, a factor of one hundred pulls the shielded-side boundary in tenfold. Use one whenever the geometry allows. Your dose record will show the difference. The source changer is the shielded container used to swap a decayed source for a fresh one — for iridium-192, with its seventy-four-day half-life, roughly every quarter. For a few seconds during that swap, only procedure stands between you and a bare hundred-curie source. Survey everything on receipt. Crank the old source into the changer and lock it. Verify the new pigtail's connection before cranking. Then survey, lock, and update the label. The meter proves every step.

sections.heading: The Collimator: Shielding You Can Aim
sections.body:
Every gamma exposure starts with a trade: the source must leave its shield to make the radiograph, and the moment it does, it radiates in **every direction** — yet the film only needs the narrow cone that passes through the weld. A **collimator** closes that gap. It is a block of dense shielding — almost always **tungsten**, sometimes lead for lower-energy sources such as selenium-75 — attached at the end of the guide tube, surrounding the exposure position. A machined **beam port** leaves open only the solid angle the radiograph actually requires: the source is cranked into the collimator, the beam exits the port at full strength, and every other direction sees the source through 12 to 25 millimeters of tungsten.

Two working styles cover most jobs:

- **Directional collimators** open on one side only — the everyday choice for shooting a weld onto film or a digital panel placed behind it.
- **Panoramic collimators** confine the beam to a flat 360-degree disc — for a source positioned at the center of a pipe, exposing a full circumferential weld in one shot while shielding everything above and below the weld plane.

A collimator weighs anywhere from a few pounds to more than twenty, and it earns every ounce: during the exposure it is the only shielding on the job sitting **at the source itself**, where shielding does the most good.

sections.caption: Cross-section of a directional collimator: the source is cranked into a tungsten body whose port passes the full-strength beam while every other direction is heavily attenuated. Inset: a panoramic collimator confines the beam to a flat 360-degree disc for circumferential pipe welds.

sections.heading: What Collimation Buys: Run the Numbers
sections.body:
Iridium-192's half-value layer in tungsten is roughly **3 millimeters**, so collimator walls of 12 to 25 mm stack four to eight HVLs — reduction factors from about **×16 to well over ×100** in every direction the port does not face. Put that on a real job.

A fresh **100 Ci Ir-192** source (about 0.5 R/h per curie at one meter) exposed bare produces roughly **50 R/h at 1 m**, 500 mR/h at 10 m, and does not fall to a 2 mR/h boundary level until about **158 m** away — a controlled circle over 300 meters across. Drop the same source into a collimator that cuts the shielded directions by a factor of 100:

- **At 1 m on the shielded side:** about 500 mR/h instead of 50 R/h.
- **Boundary on the shielded sides:** dose rate falls with the square of distance, so a ×100 reduction pulls the boundary in by ×10 — from about 158 m to about **16 m**.
- **The radiographer at the crank,** 15 m out on the shielded side for a 3-minute shot, receives roughly a tenth of a millirem instead of about 11 mR. Across thirty shots a day, that is the difference between a routine dose record and approaching annual limits in a couple of weeks.

Two bonuses come free. Less radiation flying sideways means **less scatter** reaching the film, so fog drops and contrast improves. And while no federal rule lists a collimator on every shot, your license commitments, your written operating procedures, and **ALARA** make collimation the default whenever the geometry allows it.

sections.caption: Dose-rate map of the same 100 Ci Ir-192 source bare and collimated. A ×100 reduction in the shielded directions pulls the 2 mR/h boundary from about 158 m in to about 16 m — everywhere except the direction the port faces.

sections.title: The beam port is wide open
sections.body: A collimator attenuates everything **except the beam**. Through the port, the dose rate is essentially what a bare source delivers — lethal territory close to the device. Plan the beam's direction before the shot: into the part, into the ground or a dense wall where possible, never sweeping the crew, the crank position, or occupied areas. And the boundary is still set by the **survey meter**, never by the collimator's spec sheet — scatter wraps around every shield, and the beam direction demands the same full-distance respect as an uncollimated source.

sections.heading: Source Changers: Why Sources Get Exchanged
sections.body:
Iridium-192 decays with a **74-day half-life**: every two and a half months the activity halves and exposure times double, until production becomes impractical. A working camera therefore receives a fresh source — commonly on the order of **100 curies** — roughly **every quarter**. Selenium-75, with a half-life near 120 days, stretches that cycle; cobalt-60, at 5.27 years, makes exchanges rare events.

The tool for the swap is the **source changer** — defined in 10 CFR 34.3 as a device designed and used for replacement of sealed sources in radiographic exposure devices, including changers also used for transporting and storing sealed sources. Physically it is a shielded block — depleted uranium or tungsten in a rugged housing — with one or two **shielded ports**, each with its own securing mechanism and lock, plus fittings that accept the standard drive cable and a transfer tube. Most changers are also certified **Type B transport packages**: the same container that delivers the fresh source carries the decayed one back to the supplier.

Three regulatory anchors before anyone touches one:

- **Authorization.** Source exchanges may be performed only when the license specifically authorizes them — many licensees leave exchanges to the source supplier's service technicians.
- **Locking — 10 CFR 34.23.** A changer containing a sealed source is kept locked except when under the direct surveillance of qualified personnel.
- **Inspection and maintenance — 10 CFR 34.31.** Source changers ride the same program as cameras: checked for obvious defects before use, and formally inspected and maintained at intervals not to exceed three months.

sections.caption: A source exchange in progress: the decayed source is cranked from the camera through the transfer tube into the changer's receiving port, the drive cable is then verified onto the fresh source's pigtail, and the new source is cranked home — with a survey proving every step.

sections.heading: The Exchange, Step by Step
sections.body:
An exchange is the one routine operation where a high-activity source deliberately leaves its shield twice. The sequence below condenses a typical manufacturer procedure — yours governs.

1. **Set up like an exposure.** Restricted area established and posted, full dosimetry worn — badge, direct-reading dosimeter, alarming ratemeter per 10 CFR 34.47 — and a calibrated, operable survey meter in hand.
2. **Survey on receipt.** Meter the changer and the camera, verify readings against the shipping papers, and confirm serial numbers match the source certificate.
3. **Old source out.** Connect the drive cable, then the transfer tube from the camera's outlet to the changer's empty port. Unlock and crank the decayed source into the changer until it seats. **Survey** the camera, the full tube, and the changer to prove where the source actually is — then secure the port and lock it.
4. **Reconnect.** Disconnect the drive cable from the old pigtail and connect it to the new source's pigtail. **Verify engagement** exactly per the manufacturer's procedure, including go/no-go gauge checks where specified. A false connection here is how sources end up stranded, unshielded, in a guide tube.
5. **New source home.** Crank the fresh source into the camera until it locks. Survey the camera over its whole surface — a fully shielded device reads within its posted limits, 200 mR/h at the surface — plus the tube and the changer. Surveys at every source exchange are explicitly required by 10 CFR 34.49.
6. **Close out.** Lock the changer — a 'decayed' source is still tens of curies. Update the camera's **source label and records** so the tag matches the source actually inside, and prepare the return shipment under DOT regulations.

The fields involved are unforgiving: a 100-curie source in transit through a transfer tube is a bare source a meter or two from your hands. The meter never leaves the job.

sections.caption: The source exchange, checkpoint by checkpoint — what happens and what must be proven before moving on.
headers: What must be proven — and how
rows: Changer arrives with the fresh source
Survey readings match a healthy shielded package; serial numbers match the paperwork
Boundaries posted; dosimetry on; cable and transfer tube connected
Area reads as expected before any source moves; connections made per the manufacturer
Decayed source cranked from camera into the changer's empty port
Meter shows camera and tube clean and the source seated in the changer; port secured and locked
Drive cable moved from old pigtail to the new source's pigtail
Connector fully engaged — manufacturer's go/no-go check passed before any cranking
Fresh source cranked into the camera; device lock engages
Whole-surface survey within device limits (200 mR/h at the surface); tube and changer clean
Camera's source tag matches the new source; changer locked; final area survey normal

figures.title: Inside a directional collimator (with panoramic inset)

Source changer and the exchange sequence

interactive.title: Source Exchange Day: Make the Call
interactive.intro: It is exchange day. A source changer carrying your fresh 100 Ci iridium-192 source has just arrived, and your camera holds the decayed source it is replacing. You have a calibrated survey meter, full dosimetry, and the manufacturer's procedure. Work the exchange the way a safe radiographer does.

steps.prompt: The changer comes off the truck. Before connecting anything — what comes first?
options: Open the changer's port covers and visually confirm the new source is inside
Survey the changer and your camera with the meter and check the readings against the shipping papers and expected values
Connect the transfer tube right away so the swap is staged before the crew arrives
Weigh the package to confirm a source was actually shipped
steps.feedback: Surveys on receipt prove the shielding survived transport and that the package holds what the paperwork claims. Eyes cannot see dose — you never open port covers to look. Every exchange starts and ends with the meter.

steps.prompt: You crank the decayed source out of the camera, through the transfer tube, and feel it seat in the changer's empty port. What proves this step is complete?
options: The crank reached its stop, which means the source is in the changer
The number of crank turns matched the manufacturer's figure
A survey of the changer, the full transfer tube, and the camera shows the source seated and shielded in the changer — then you secure the port and lock it
The pigtail tag is no longer visible at the camera
steps.feedback: Crank feel and turn counts are mechanics, not proof — cables jam and connectors let go. The meter walked along the tube and held at both shields is the only verification that the source is where you think it is. Then the securing mechanism and lock make it stay there.

steps.prompt: Before cranking the fresh source out of the changer into your camera, the one verification you never skip:
options: The drive-cable connector is fully and correctly engaged with the new source's pigtail, checked per the manufacturer's procedure
The new source's activity exactly matches the old source's original activity
The transfer tube is freshly lubricated so the source moves quickly
Your partner is standing at the changer to watch the source come out
steps.feedback: A false connection is the classic exchange accident: the source disconnects mid-transfer and is stranded, bare, in the tube. Verify engagement per the manufacturer — go/no-go gauge where specified — before the source moves an inch. And nobody stands at the changer: that is an open port with a naked source about to pass through it.

steps.prompt: The new source cranks home and the camera lock engages. What closes out the exchange and returns the device to service?
options: Nothing further — the lock engaging is proof the exchange is complete
A firm shake of the camera to confirm the source is not loose
Stowing the changer unlocked, since the decayed source inside is spent
Surveying the camera's whole surface, the tube, and the changer; locking the changer; updating the device's source label and records; preparing the decayed source's return shipment
steps.feedback: 10 CFR 34.49 requires a survey whenever a source is exchanged, and 10 CFR 34.23 keeps the changer locked — a 'decayed' source is still tens of curies. The device label must match the source actually inside it, and the old source travels back as a Type B shipment under DOT rules. Locks and crank feel are never proof; the meter is.

checkQuestions.question: How does a collimator reduce personnel exposure during a radiographic exposure?
options: It attenuates the primary beam so the source can stay out longer
It seals the exposure position so no radiation escapes in any direction
It surrounds the exposure position with dense shielding and leaves open only the port the radiograph needs — the beam is unattenuated while every other direction is cut by roughly ×16 to over ×100
It shortens the source's travel so it spends less time outside the camera
checkQuestions.explanation: A collimator is aimed shielding: the working beam passes through the open port at full strength, and the tungsten body attenuates every other direction, collapsing dose rates and boundary distances on the shielded sides.

checkQuestions.question: A bare 100 Ci Ir-192 source produces about 50 R/h at 1 m. Inside a collimator providing a ×100 reduction, the dose rate at 1 m on the shielded side is about:
options: 500 mR/h
5 R/h
50 mR/h
essentially zero
checkQuestions.explanation: 50 R/h divided by 100 is 0.5 R/h — 500 mR/h. Shielding attenuates in fractions; it never reduces a field to zero, which is why the boundary is still set by survey.

checkQuestions.question: Why are iridium-192 sources typically exchanged about every quarter?
options: Federal regulations set a hard 90-day service life for sealed sources
The source capsule wears out from repeated cranking
The camera's depleted-uranium shield degrades as the source ages
With a 74-day half-life, the activity halves and exposure times roughly double every two and a half months, until production becomes impractical
checkQuestions.explanation: Exchange timing is driven by decay, not by a regulatory expiration: as Ir-192 activity falls, exposure times stretch until the source no longer supports production, so fresh sources arrive roughly quarterly.

checkQuestions.question: During a source exchange, before cranking the fresh source out of the changer, the critical verification is that:
options: the changer's gross weight matches the shipping papers
the drive-cable connector is fully and properly engaged with the new source's pigtail, per the manufacturer's procedure
the new source has the same activity as the old source did when new
the transfer tube has been lubricated so the source moves smoothly
checkQuestions.explanation: A false connection can let the source disconnect mid-transfer and strand it, unshielded, in the tube — the classic exchange accident. Engagement is verified per the manufacturer, including go/no-go gauge checks where specified.

checkQuestions.question: After the new source is cranked into the camera and the lock engages, 10 CFR Part 34 requires that:
options: no survey is needed if the lock engaged on the first try
the device may return to service once the crank reaches its stop
a survey with a calibrated, operable instrument confirms the source is fully shielded — and the changer holding the decayed source is kept locked
the changer may be left unlocked while awaiting return shipment, since the old source has decayed
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.49 requires surveys whenever a source is exchanged — locks and crank feel are never proof of shielding — and 10 CFR 34.23 keeps source changers containing sealed sources locked; a decayed source is still tens of curies.

references: 10 CFR Part 34 — Licenses for Industrial Radiography and Radiation Safety Requirements (§34.3 source changer definition; §34.23 locking of devices, containers, and source changers; §34.31 inspection and maintenance; §34.47 personnel monitoring; §34.49 radiation surveys)
ANSI/ASNT CP-105 (2024) — Radiation Safety topical outline §9.5–9.6 (use of collimators to reduce personnel exposure; use of source changers for gamma-ray sources)
ANSI N432 — Radiological Safety for the Design and Construction of Apparatus for Gamma Radiography (equipment performance standard incorporated by 10 CFR 34.20)
49 CFR Parts 171–178 — U.S. DOT Hazardous Materials Regulations (Type B packaging and shipment of returned sealed sources)
